flashcards.front: What is economics?
flashcards.back: Economics is a social science concerned with understanding the production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services. More specifically, the focus of economics is on how consumers, businesses, and governments make choices when allocating resources to satisfy their needs. The sum of those choices determines what happens in the economy.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between microeconomics and macroeconomics?
flashcards.back:
Microeconomics analyzes the market behaviour of individual consumers and firms, how prices are determined, how price determines the production/distribution/use of goods and services, how consumers decide how much of various goods to purchase, workers decide what jobs to take, and firms decide how many workers to hire and how much output to produce.

Macroeconomics focuses on the performance of the economy as a whole — the challenges facing society due to limited natural resources, human effort, and technology. It focuses on unemployment, inflation, recessions, government spending/taxation, poverty and inequality, budget deficits and national debts.

flashcards.front: Who are the three decision makers in the economy?
flashcards.back:
1. Consumers — set out to maximize their satisfaction or well-being within the limitations of their available resources (income from employment, investments, or other sources).
2. Firms — set out to maximize profits by selling their goods or services to consumers, governments, or other firms.
3. Governments — spend money on education, health care, employment training, and the military; oversee regulatory agencies; take part in public works projects (highways, hydro-electric plants, airports).

flashcards.front: What is a market?
flashcards.back: A market is any arrangement that allows buyers and sellers to conduct business with one another. For example, the fixed-income market is a network of investment professionals, distribution channels, suppliers, and wholesalers who develop and trade products to meet various investor needs. These decision makers do not meet physically, but they are connected and make their deals by a variety of electronic means. Ultimately, this organized marketplace allows participants access to a product that investors want to buy or sell.

flashcards.front: What are the Laws of Demand and Supply?
flashcards.back:
Law of Demand: The quantity demanded of a good or service is the total amount consumers are willing to buy at a particular price during a given time period. The higher the price, the lower the quantity demanded; the lower the price, the higher the quantity demanded — all other factors held constant.

Law of Supply: The quantity supplied of a good or service is the total amount that producers are willing to supply at a particular price during a given time period. The higher the price of a good, the greater the quantity supplied.

flashcards.front: What is market equilibrium?
flashcards.back:
Market equilibrium is the price that matches what someone is willing to pay for a product with the price at which someone is willing to supply it — it is the price where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.

Example: In a fictitious laptop market, equilibrium occurs at a price of $2,000 and a quantity of 200 units. At higher prices, supply exceeds demand; at lower prices, demand exceeds supply. The intersection of the supply and demand curves yields the equilibrium price and quantity.

flashcards.front: What is Gross Domestic Product (GDP)?
flashcards.back:
Gross domestic product (GDP) is the market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given time period, usually a year or a quarter. Quarterly reports are used to keep track of short-term activity, while annual reports examine trends and changes in production and the standard of living.

A final good is a finished product purchased by the ultimate end user. GDP looks at the total amount of final goods produced over the period — intermediate goods (components used in production) are not counted separately to avoid double-counting.

flashcards.front: What are the three approaches to measuring GDP?
flashcards.back:
1. Expenditure Approach: GDP = C + I + G + (X − M)
   • C = Personal consumption
   • I = Investment
   • G = Government spending on goods and services
   • X − M = Net exports (exports minus imports)

2. Income Approach: GDP equals the total income generated by producing all goods and services (sum of all income earned).

3. Production Approach (value-added): Also known as the value-added approach; calculates an industry's output and subtracts the value of all goods and services used in production. In theory, all three measures produce the same GDP figure.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between nominal GDP and real GDP?
flashcards.back:
Nominal GDP: The value of all goods and services produced in a given year at prices that prevailed in that same year. It is typically the amount reported in financial press. Changes in nominal GDP from year to year reflect both changes in prices and changes in the amount of output produced.

Real GDP (constant dollar GDP): The dollar value of all goods and services produced in a given year at prices from a base year. Holding prices constant to a base year gives a better measure of changes in the amount of output produced during the year — it removes the effect of inflation from GDP figures.

flashcards.front: What are the five phases of the business cycle?
flashcards.back:
1. Expansion: stable inflation, businesses investing to meet higher demand, rising corporate profits, job creation steady, rising real GDP.
2. Peak: demand outstrips economy's capacity, labour/product shortages cause wage increases and inflation, interest rates rise, business sales decline.
3. Contraction: economic activity declines, real GDP decreases, businesses reduce production, lay off workers, consumer confidence falls. If it lasts at least two consecutive quarters, it is a recession.
4. Trough: growth cycle reaches its lowest point, interest rates fall triggering a bond rally, inflation falls, stock prices begin to rally.
5. Recovery: GDP returns to its previous peak, renewed buying of interest rate-sensitive items, businesses rebuild inventories, unemployment remains high.

flashcards.front: What are leading, coincident, and lagging economic indicators?
flashcards.back:
Leading indicators: peak and trough before the overall economy; anticipate emerging trends. Examples: housing starts, manufacturers' new orders, commodity prices, average hours worked per week, stock prices, money supply.

Coincident indicators: change at approximately the same time and in the same direction as the whole economy; provide information about the current state. Examples: personal income, GDP, industrial production, retail sales.

Lagging indicators: change after the economy has changed; confirm that a business cycle pattern is occurring. Examples: unemployment, private sector plant and equipment spending, business loans and interest, labour costs, inflation rate.

flashcards.front: How is a recession defined?
flashcards.back:
A popular definition of a recession is at least two consecutive quarters of declining real GDP.

However, Statistics Canada judges a recession by the depth, duration, and diffusion of the decline in business activity:
• The decline must be of substantial depth (marginal declines may be statistical error)
• The duration must be more than a couple of months (bad weather alone can cause a temporary decline)
• The decline must be a feature of the whole economy (not just one sector)
• The behaviour of employment and per capita income may also be taken into account

Recent periods in Canada: April–September 2001 (5 months), July 2008–July 2009 (12 months), March–August 2020 (6 months).

flashcards.front: What is a soft landing?
flashcards.back: A soft landing describes a business cycle phase when economic growth slows sharply but does not turn negative, while inflation falls or remains low. Soft landings are considered the 'Holy Grail' of policy makers, who want sustained growth without the cost of recurring recessions.

flashcards.front: What are the two key labour market indicators?
flashcards.back:
1. Participation rate: represents the share of the working-age population (those aged 15 and older) that is in the labour force. For Canada, the current participation rate is 65.4% (Source: Statistics Canada Labour Force Survey). It shows the willingness of people to enter the workforce and take jobs.

2. Unemployment rate: represents the share of the labour force that is unemployed and actively looking for work. It may rise either because the number of employed fell or the number of people entering the work force looking for work rose, or both. The unemployment rate in Canada was 5.8% in December 2023.

flashcards.front: What are the four types of unemployment?
flashcards.back:
1. Cyclical unemployment: tied directly to fluctuations in the business cycle; rises when the economy weakens and drops when the economy strengthens.
2. Seasonal unemployment: occurs in industries that operate only during part of the year (e.g., farmhands hired June to October; winter months bring seasonal unemployment in such industries).
3. Frictional unemployment: result of normal labour turnover — people entering and leaving the workforce, from ongoing creation and destruction of jobs. People are looking for work after finishing school, quitting, being laid off, or being fired from their most recent job. This is a normal part of a healthy economy.
4. Structural unemployment: occurs when workers are unable to find work because they lack necessary skills, do not live where jobs are available, or decide not to work at the wage rate offered by the market. Typically lasts longer than frictional unemployment.

flashcards.front: What is the natural unemployment rate?
flashcards.back:
The natural unemployment rate is the minimal level of unemployment that will not drop to zero because of frictional and structural factors in a healthy economy. At this level, the economy is thought to be operating at close to its full potential or capacity such that all resources, including labour, are fully employed.

The Bank of Canada pays close attention to the gap between the actual unemployment rate and the natural rate:
• When actual unemployment is above the natural rate, an excess supply of workers weakens labour bargaining power, which discourages wage gains and helps keep inflation in check.
• When actual unemployment is below the natural rate, a shortage of workers contributes to higher wages and higher inflation.

flashcards.front: What are the determinants of interest rates?
flashcards.back:
A broad range of factors influences interest rates:
1. Demand and supply of capital: large government deficits or boom in business investment raises demand for capital, forcing up interest rates; increased savings may reduce them.
2. Default risk: greater risk of borrower default → higher interest rate demanded; additional rate to compensate is called a default premium.
3. Foreign interest rates and the exchange rate: since Canada has an open economy, foreign interest rates and financial conditions influence Canadian rates.
4. Central bank credibility: central bank exercises influence by raising/lowering short-term rates; more credible, long-established commitment to low inflation → lower interest rates.
5. Inflation: the higher the expected inflation rate, the higher the interest rate that must be charged by lenders to compensate for erosion of purchasing power.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between the nominal interest rate and the real interest rate?
flashcards.back:
Nominal interest rate: the quoted rate on a loan or investment; reflects the effects of inflation included.

Real interest rate: the nominal interest rate minus the expected inflation rate. It represents the actual return on an investment after removing the impact of inflation.

Formula: Real interest rate = Nominal interest rate − Expected inflation rate

Example: If a bank charges 5% on a loan and expected inflation is 2%, the real interest rate is 3%. The real rate better reflects the true cost of borrowing or true return on investment.

flashcards.front: What is the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and how is the inflation rate calculated?
flashcards.back:
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is one of the most widely used indicators of inflation in Canada. It measures the cost of living by tracking the retail price of a shopping basket comprised of 600 different goods and services, each weighted to reflect typical consumer spending. The base year is 2002 (CPI = 100). The CPI was 158.3 at the end of December 2023, indicating that the basket costs 58.3% more than it did in 2002.

Inflation Rate Formula:
Inflation Rate = (CPI Current Period − CPI Previous Period) / CPI Previous Period × 100

Example: CPI December 2023 = 158.3; CPI December 2022 = 153.1
Inflation Rate = (158.3 − 153.1) / 153.1 × 100 = 3.4%

flashcards.front: What are the costs of inflation?
flashcards.back:
Inflation imposes many costs on the economy:
1. Erodes the standard of living for those on a fixed income and those who lack wage bargaining power — rewards those able to increase their income either through wages or changes to their investment strategy.
2. Reduces the real value of investments such as fixed-rate loans, since the loans are paid back in dollars that buy less. This can be good for the borrower, but bad for the lender.
3. Distorts the signal prices send to participants in market economies — rising prices draw resources into areas of scarcity and falling prices move funds away from glutted areas. When inflation is high, it is difficult to tell if a price increase is truly inflationary or a genuine relative price change.
4. Accelerating inflation usually brings about rising interest rates and a recession — high-inflation economies usually experience more severe booms and busts than low-inflation economies.

flashcards.front: What is the output gap, and how does it relate to inflation?
flashcards.back:
The output gap is the difference between real GDP (what the economy actually produces) and potential GDP (what the economy is capable of producing when all inputs of labour, capital, and technology are fully employed at their normal levels).

Negative output gap (actual < potential): spare capacity in the economy — unemployed workers and unused plant/equipment. Inflation will fall or remain steady.

Positive output gap (actual > potential): the economy is trying to produce more than it can with existing resources. Scarse labour fuels wage increases, and other strains on productive resources place upward pressure on inflation — this is demand-pull inflation.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between demand-pull and cost-push inflation?
flashcards.back:
Demand-pull inflation: Occurs as the economy expands through an expansion toward the peak. Output continues to expand, consumer income is rising, and this leads to strong consumer demand for goods and services. Companies can continue to operate well above normal capacity; strong demand means companies can raise prices higher — consumer demand is 'pulling' prices higher.

Cost-push inflation: Occurs when the cost of producing output changes from the supply side — when higher production costs lead firms to raise their prices. Higher costs of raw materials cause firms to respond by raising prices and producing a smaller amount of the product. This is an example of costs 'pushing' inflation higher.

flashcards.front: What is disinflation vs. deflation?
flashcards.back:
Disinflation: A decline in the rate at which prices rise — a decrease in the rate of inflation. Prices are still rising, but at a slower rate. The Phillips curve says that when unemployment is low, inflation tends to be high, and vice versa. Bringing down inflation 1% (the sacrifice ratio) may require GDP to be reduced by as much as 4% — a considerable cost in output.

Deflation: A sustained fall in prices where the annual change in the CPI is negative year after year. Falling prices are generally good for the economy in the short term, but a sustained fall in prices can have negative implications: businesses must sell at lower prices and lower wages → declining company earnings → economic slowdown → falling stock prices.

flashcards.front: What is monetary policy and how does the Bank of Canada implement it?
flashcards.back:
Monetary policy refers to regulation of the money supply and available credit for the purpose of promoting sustained economic growth and price stability. The Bank of Canada uses its influence over short-term interest rates to control changes in the money supply and available credit.

Monetary policy can be:
• Expansionary: increases money and credit available in the economy (Bank lowers Bank Rate or buys bonds → borrowing cheaper → more loans → money supply increases)
• Contractionary: decreases money and credit available in the economy (Bank raises Bank Rate or sells bonds → borrowing expensive → fewer loans → money supply decreases)

Tools used: the Bank Rate, cash management (open-market operations), and drawdowns and redeposits.

flashcards.front: What is the Bank Rate and the overnight rate?
flashcards.back:
The Bank of Canada carries out monetary policy primarily through changes to what it calls the target for the overnight rate. The overnight rate is the interest rate set in the overnight market — a marketplace where major Canadian financial institutions lend each other money on an overnight basis. When the Bank changes the target for the overnight rate, other short-term interest rates also usually change.

The Bank Rate is the upper limit of the overnight rate operating band, which is 50 basis points (0.5 percentage point) wide. The Bank Rate is the rate at which the Bank of Canada will lend money on a last resort to chartered banks and other members of Payments Canada. If the operating band is 1%–1.5%, the Bank Rate is 1.5%.

As of February 2024, the Bank Rate was at 5.0%. It had been maintained at 0.50% for more than a year during the 2008–2009 recession.

flashcards.front: What is fiscal policy and what are its two types?
flashcards.back:
Fiscal policy is a deliberate action by the government (federal, provincial, or territorial) to influence the economy through changes either in spending or in taxation initiatives.

Expansionary fiscal policy: implemented when the government believes current economic growth is lower than a specific target level; may increase spending or decrease taxes to stimulate the economy.
Example: If unemployment is too high, the government might cut business taxes to encourage businesses to hire more workers.

Contractionary fiscal policy: implemented when the government believes that current economic growth is rising too quickly and wants to cool the economy; may decrease spending or increase taxes.
Example: If public spending is too high, the government might cut public programs, such as health care spending, even though such cuts may increase unemployment.

flashcards.front: What is the balance of payments?
flashcards.back:
The balance of payments is a detailed statement of a country's economic transactions with the rest of the world for a given period, typically over a quarter or a year. It has two main components:

1. Current account: records the exchanges of goods and services between Canadians and foreigners, the earnings from investment income, and net transfers (such as foreign aid). The most important component is merchandise trade — goods and services produced and sold abroad, and those imported from other countries.

2. Capital and financial account: records financial flows between Canadians and foreigners related to investments by foreigners in Canada and investments by Canadians abroad. The key difference from current account transactions is that capital/financial account transactions result in the acquisition of an asset and the right to any income it earns.

fillInTheBlank.sentence: ___ analyzes the market behaviour of individual consumers and firms, how prices are determined, while ___ focuses on the performance of the economy as a whole.
The three decision makers in the economy are consumers, ___, and governments.
fillInTheBlank.hint: Businesses that aim to maximize profits.

fillInTheBlank.sentence: GDP is calculated using the expenditure approach as GDP = C + ___ + G + (X − M).
___ GDP holds prices constant to a base year, removing the effects of inflation, while ___ GDP reflects prices in the year the output was produced.
fillInTheBlank.hint: One removes inflation; the other includes it.

fillInTheBlank.sentence: When an economy passes its peak and enters a downturn, if the contraction lasts at least two consecutive quarters, then the economy is considered to be in ___.
fillInTheBlank.hint: The economic term for a significant decline in activity.

fillInTheBlank.sentence: ___ indicators peak and trough before the overall economy, while ___ indicators change after the economy has changed.
fillInTheBlank.hint: One predicts the future; the other confirms the past.

fillInTheBlank.sentence: The ___ rate is the share of the working-age population that is in the labour force, while the ___ rate is the share of the labour force that is unemployed and actively looking for work.
fillInTheBlank.hint: Two key measures of the Canadian labour market.

fillInTheBlank.sentence: Inflation Rate = (CPI Current Period − CPI Previous Period) ÷ CPI Previous Period × ___.
fillInTheBlank.hint: You multiply by this number to express the result as a percentage.

fillInTheBlank.sentence: The ___ interest rate is the quoted rate charged on a loan, while the ___ interest rate equals the nominal rate minus the expected inflation rate.
fillInTheBlank.hint: One includes inflation; the other strips it out.

fillInTheBlank.sentence: Monetary policy can be expansionary or ___. An expansionary policy increases the money supply, while a contractionary policy decreases it.
fillInTheBlank.hint: The opposite of expansionary — it contracts the money supply.

trueFalse.statement: Microeconomics focuses on the performance of the economy as a whole, including unemployment and inflation.
trueFalse.explanation: False. That describes macroeconomics. Microeconomics analyzes the market behaviour of individual consumers and firms — how prices are determined, how consumers decide what to buy, and how firms decide how many workers to hire and how much to produce.

trueFalse.statement: According to the Law of Demand, the higher the price of a good, the lower the quantity demanded.
trueFalse.explanation: True. The Law of Demand states that when the price of a good rises, the quantity that consumers are willing to buy falls, all other factors held constant. This inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded is one of the fundamental principles of economics.

trueFalse.statement: A final good is any product used in the production of another product.
trueFalse.explanation: False. A final good is a finished product purchased by the ultimate end user. Products used in the production of other products are intermediate goods and are not counted separately in GDP to avoid double-counting. For example, the chip inside a computer is an intermediate good; the computer itself is the final good.

trueFalse.statement: Real GDP is a better measure of changes in output because it removes the effect of price changes from year to year.
trueFalse.explanation: True. Real GDP (or constant dollar GDP) holds prices constant to a base year, so changes in real GDP reflect only changes in the quantity of goods and services produced — not changes in prices. This makes it a more accurate measure of economic growth.

trueFalse.statement: The contraction phase of the business cycle is characterized by rising corporate profits, steady job creation, and increasing real GDP.
trueFalse.explanation: False. Those characteristics describe the expansion phase. During contraction, the level of economic activity declines (real GDP decreases), businesses reduce production and may lay off employees, business failures outnumber start-ups, and falling employment erodes household income and confidence.

trueFalse.statement: The unemployment rate is one of the most popular lagging economic indicators.
trueFalse.explanation: True. Unemployment is a lagging indicator — it changes after the economy as a whole has changed. A rising unemployment rate is an indication that the economy is doing poorly or that companies are anticipating a downturn. Businesses tend to hire only after they are confident the economy is recovering, so unemployment peaks well after the recession ends.

trueFalse.statement: Frictional unemployment occurs when workers are unable to find work because they lack necessary skills or do not live where jobs are available.
trueFalse.explanation: False. That describes structural unemployment. Frictional unemployment is the result of normal labour turnover — people entering and leaving the workforce from the ongoing creation and destruction of jobs. People look for work after finishing school, quitting, being laid off, or being fired. This is a normal part of a healthy economy.

trueFalse.statement: The Consumer Price Index (CPI) uses a base year of 2002, which was assigned a value of 100.
trueFalse.explanation: True. When calculating the CPI, prices are measured against a base year, which is 2002 in Canada, and is given a value of 100. The total CPI was 158.3 at the end of December 2023, indicating that the basket of goods costs 58.3% more than it did in 2002.

trueFalse.statement: A negative output gap means the economy is producing above its potential, which tends to push inflation higher.
trueFalse.explanation: False. A negative output gap means actual output is below potential output — there is spare capacity in the economy (unemployed workers, unused plant/equipment). In this case, inflation will fall or remain steady. A positive output gap (economy above potential) is what tends to push inflation higher through demand-pull inflation.

trueFalse.statement: An expansionary monetary policy by the Bank of Canada involves lowering the Bank Rate or buying bonds in the market.
trueFalse.explanation: True. An expansionary monetary policy increases the amount of money and credit available in the economy. If the Bank of Canada lowers the Bank Rate or buys bonds in the market, borrowing will become cheaper and spending will be more attractive. As a result, more loans will be made and less money will be tied up in investments, increasing the money supply.

trueFalse.statement: Deflation is beneficial to the economy in the long term because falling prices make goods and services more affordable.
trueFalse.explanation: False. While falling prices may seem good in the short term, a sustained deflation has serious negative implications: businesses must sell at lower prices, leading to declining corporate profits. Businesses cut production costs and may wage rates, which worsens economic slowdown and causes a decline in stock prices. Ultimately, declining company earnings trigger economic recession.

trueFalse.statement: The current account records financial flows related to investments by foreigners in Canada and investments by Canadians abroad.
trueFalse.explanation: False. That describes the capital and financial account. The current account records the exchanges of goods and services between Canadians and foreigners, earnings from investment income, and net transfers (such as foreign aid). Its most important component is merchandise trade.

multipleChoice.question: What is the focus of macroeconomics?
options: How individual consumers decide what products to buy
How individual firms determine their prices and output levels
The performance of the economy as a whole, including unemployment, inflation, and growth
The behaviour of financial markets and individual stock prices
multipleChoice.explanation: Macroeconomics focuses on the performance of the economy as a whole. It looks at the broader picture and the challenges facing society as a result of the limited amounts of natural resources, human effort, and skills. It deals with unemployment, inflation, recessions, government spending and taxation, poverty, budget deficits, and national debts.

multipleChoice.question: In the laptop market example from Table 3.1, what is the equilibrium price and quantity?
options: $1,000 and 500 units
$1,500 and 350 units
$2,000 and 200 units
$3,000 and 450 units
multipleChoice.explanation: The equilibrium price is $2,000, at which quantity demanded (200 units) equals quantity supplied (200 units). At any higher price, supply exceeds demand; at any lower price, demand exceeds supply. The intersection of the supply and demand curves at $2,000 and 200 units yields market equilibrium.

multipleChoice.question: Which GDP measurement approach uses the formula GDP = C + I + G + (X − M)?
options: Income approach
Production approach
Expenditure approach
Value-added approach
multipleChoice.explanation: The expenditure approach calculates GDP by adding up all spending: Personal Consumption (C), Investment (I), Government spending on goods and services (G), and Net Exports (X − M, i.e., exports minus imports). This is the most widely used approach and the one most commonly reported in the financial press.

multipleChoice.question: During which phase of the business cycle does demand begin to outstrip the economy's capacity, causing wage increases and rising inflation?
options: Trough
Recovery
Expansion
Peak
multipleChoice.explanation: At the Peak of the business cycle, demand begins to outstrip the capacity of the economy to supply it. Labour and product shortages cause wage increases and inflation to rise. Interest rates rise, bond prices fall, business sales decline, and stock prices and market activity decline.

multipleChoice.question: Which of the following is a leading economic indicator?
options: The unemployment rate
Business loans and interest on such borrowing
Housing starts
Personal income
multipleChoice.explanation: Housing starts is a leading indicator — it peaks and troughs before the overall economy. When people are building more houses, it signals strong economic expectations. Unemployment and business loans are lagging indicators (change after the economy). Personal income is a coincident indicator (changes at the same time as the economy).

multipleChoice.question: What does Statistics Canada use to identify a recession (rather than the simple two-quarter rule)?
options: A decline of at least 3% in nominal GDP over one quarter
The depth, duration, and diffusion of the decline in business activity
A rise in the unemployment rate of more than 2 percentage points
A sustained decline in the stock market of more than 20%
multipleChoice.explanation: Statistics Canada judges a recession by the depth (the decline must be substantial, not marginal), duration (more than a couple of months), and diffusion (it must be a feature of the whole economy, not just one sector). The behaviour of employment and per capita income may also be taken into account.

multipleChoice.question: Which type of unemployment occurs because workers lack necessary skills or do not live where jobs are available?
options: Cyclical unemployment
Seasonal unemployment
Frictional unemployment
Structural unemployment
multipleChoice.explanation: Structural unemployment occurs when workers are unable to find work or fill available jobs because they lack the necessary skills, do not live where jobs are available, or decide not to work at the wage rate offered by the market. It is closely tied to changes in technology, international competition, and government policy. It typically lasts longer than frictional unemployment.

multipleChoice.question: If the CPI in December 2022 was 153.1 and in December 2023 was 158.3, what was the annual inflation rate?
multipleChoice.explanation: Inflation Rate = (CPI Current − CPI Previous) / CPI Previous × 100 = (158.3 − 153.1) / 153.1 × 100 = 5.2 / 153.1 × 100 = 3.4%. The inflation rate over the 12-month period was 3.4%, meaning the basket of goods cost 3.4% more in December 2023 than in December 2022.

multipleChoice.question: What is the real interest rate?
options: The interest rate set by the Bank of Canada for overnight lending
The nominal interest rate minus the expected inflation rate
The interest rate that banks charge their best corporate customers
The nominal interest rate plus a default risk premium
multipleChoice.explanation: The real interest rate equals the nominal interest rate minus the expected inflation rate. While the nominal rate is the quoted rate on a loan or investment, the real rate reflects the actual return (or cost) after adjusting for inflation. For example, if the nominal rate is 5% and expected inflation is 2%, the real rate is 3%.

multipleChoice.question: What is cost-push inflation?
options: Inflation caused by strong consumer demand pushing prices higher when the economy operates above its potential
Inflation caused by an increase in the money supply
Inflation caused by higher production costs (e.g., raw materials) leading firms to raise their prices
Inflation caused by government spending on public works projects
multipleChoice.explanation: Cost-push inflation occurs when the cost of producing output changes from the supply side. When higher costs of raw materials lead firms to raise their prices and produce smaller amounts, this 'pushes' prices higher. By contrast, demand-pull inflation is driven by strong consumer demand pulling prices higher when the economy operates above potential.

multipleChoice.question: Which of the following tools does the Bank of Canada use to implement monetary policy?
options: Setting income tax rates and GST rates
The Bank Rate, cash management (open-market operations), and drawdowns/redeposits
Regulating the money supply directly by printing currency
Setting the exchange rate for the Canadian dollar
multipleChoice.explanation: The Bank of Canada implements monetary policy using three main tools: (1) the Bank Rate — the upper limit of the overnight rate operating band; (2) cash management or open-market operations — buying or selling bonds in the market; and (3) drawdowns and redeposits — transferring funds to/from the Bank of Canada and the chartered banks to influence short-term interest rates.

multipleChoice.question: What is fiscal policy?
options: Regulation of the money supply and credit conditions by the Bank of Canada
Deliberate action by the government to influence the economy through changes in spending or taxation
Setting of interest rates by the chartered banks to meet monetary targets
International trade policy managed by the federal Department of Finance
multipleChoice.explanation: Fiscal policy is a deliberate action by the government (federal, provincial, or territorial) to influence the economy through changes either in spending or in taxation initiatives. It can be expansionary (increase spending or decrease taxes to stimulate growth) or contractionary (decrease spending or increase taxes to cool an overheating economy). This is distinct from monetary policy, which is managed by the Bank of Canada.

multipleChoice.question: The balance of payments consists of two main components. What are they?
options: The monetary account and the fiscal account
The trade account and the capital account
The current account and the capital and financial account
The domestic account and the international account
multipleChoice.explanation: The balance of payments has two main components: (1) the current account — records exchanges of goods and services, earnings from investment income, and net transfers between Canada and foreign countries; and (2) the capital and financial account — records financial flows related to investments by foreigners in Canada and investments by Canadians abroad.

multipleChoice.question: Canada's participation rate is currently approximately:
multipleChoice.explanation: According to Statistics Canada's Labour Force Survey, Canada's participation rate is 65.4%. The participation rate has followed a mostly upward trend over the last 50 years, rising from 54% in the early 1960s to its current level of 65.4%. It represents the share of the working-age population (those aged 15 and older) that is in the labour force.

multipleChoice.question: During the business cycle's trough phase, which of the following typically occurs?
options: Interest rates rise, triggering a bond sell-off
Businesses have adjusted inventories and are investing in new capacity
Interest rates fall, triggering a bond rally; inflation falls; stock prices begin to rally
Corporate profits reach their peak and unemployment is at its lowest
multipleChoice.explanation: At the trough, the contraction continues and the growth cycle reaches its lowest point. Interest rates fall (triggering a bond rally), inflation falls, consumers who postponed purchases during the contraction are spurred by lower interest rates and begin to spend, and stock prices begin to rally. The trough marks the bottom before the recovery begins.

textbook.content:
Economic news and events are announced daily. There are monetary policy reports from the Bank of Canada, quarterly gross domestic product estimates, regular changes in the Canadian exchange rate relative to the U.S. dollar, and reports on monthly unemployment and housing starts to consider. For an investor or advisor, being able to recognize the impact these events could have on markets and individual investments helps make wise investment decisions.

Economics is fundamentally about understanding the choices individuals make and how the sum of those choices affects our market economy. Whether it is the purchase of groceries, a home, or stocks and bonds, the interaction between consumer choices and the economy takes place in an organized market and at a price determined by demand and supply for goods and services by consumers, investors, and governments.

An example of an organized market is the Toronto Stock Exchange. Investors come together to buy and sell securities anonymously. Millions of transactions are carried out each day, and this anonymous interaction creates a market and an equilibrium price for a variety of securities. The buyer and seller of a security both have views about the security (generally, the buyer believes it will go up in value and the seller believes it will go down), and it is likely that some type of economic analysis went into the decision to buy or sell.

Economics is a social science concerned with understanding the production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services. More specifically, the focus of economics is on how consumers, businesses, and governments make choices when allocating resources to satisfy their needs. The sum of those choices determines what happens in the economy.

Most of us would like to have more of what we have, or at least be able to buy or consume as much as we can. In reality, this is not possible because our spending habits are constrained by the amount of income we earn and by the fact that there is a limit to what an economy can produce during a given period. Because scarcity prevents us from having as much as we would like of certain goods, the performance of the economy hinges on the collective decisions made by millions of individuals. Ultimately, the interaction between these market participants determines what we pay for a good or service, or a stock or mutual fund, for example.

Economics is divided into two main topic areas: microeconomics and macroeconomics.

Microeconomics analyzes the market behaviour of individual consumers and firms, how prices are determined, and how price determines the production, distribution, and use of goods and services. For example, consumers decide how prices determine the production, distribution, and use of goods and services. Workers decide what jobs to take, and firms decide how many workers to hire and how much output to produce.

Microeconomics looks to answer questions such as:
• How do minimum wage laws affect the supply of labour and company profit margins?
• How would a tax on softwood lumber imports affect the growth prospects in the forestry industry?
• If a government placed a tax on the purchase of mutual funds, will consumers stop buying them?

Macroeconomics focuses on the performance of the economy as a whole. It looks at the broader picture and the challenges facing society as a result of the limited amounts of natural resources, human effort and skills, and technology. Whereas microeconomics looks at how the individual is impacted by changes in prices or income levels, macroeconomics focuses on such important issues as unemployment, inflation, recessions, government spending and taxation, poverty and inequality, budget deficits and national debts.

Macroeconomics looks to answer questions such as:
• Why did total output shrink last quarter?
• Why have the number of jobs fallen in the last year?
• Will a decrease in interest rates stimulate economic growth?
• How can a nation improve its standard of living?

There are three main groups that interact in the economy: consumers, firms, and governments.

Consumers: Consumers set out to maximize their satisfaction or well-being within the limitations of their available resources — income from employment, investments, or other sources.

Firms: Firms set out to maximize profits by selling their goods or services to consumers, governments, or other firms.

Governments: Governments spend money on education, health care, employment training, and the military. They oversee regulatory agencies and they take part in public works projects, including highways, hydro-electric plants, and airports.

The price of a product is probably one of the most important factors that determines how much of that product individuals will buy or sell in the marketplace. Everything has a price, and financial products and services are not exempt; stocks, bonds, commodities, currency all have visible prices that allow individuals to make investment decisions. The price paid for any product is largely determined by the demand for and supply of that product in the marketplace.

Two general economic principles help explain the interaction between demand and supply:
• Law of Demand: The quantity demanded of a good or service is the total amount consumers are willing to buy at a particular price during a given time period. The higher the price, the lower the quantity demanded, while the lower the price the higher the quantity demanded, other factors held constant.
• Law of Supply: The quantity supplied of a good or service is the total amount that producers are willing to supply at a particular price during a given time period. According to the Law of Supply, the higher the price of a good, the greater the quantity supplied.

The interaction that takes place between buyers and sellers in the market ultimately determines an equilibrium price for that product — basically, this is the price that matches what someone is willing to pay for the product with the price at which someone is willing to supply it.

For example, we can find the market equilibrium of a fictitious laptop market using the information from Table 3.1:

Table 3.1 | Market for Laptops
Price    | Quantity Demanded | Quantity Supplied
$1,000   | 500               | 0
$1,500   | 350               | 100
$2,000   | 200               | 200
$2,500   | 150               | 300
$3,000   | 10                | 450

The one price that ensures a balance between the quantity demanded and the quantity supplied is $2,000. This intersection yields an equilibrium price of $2,000 and an equilibrium supply of 200 units.

Demand and supply forces are instrumental in regulating the price of financial instruments. For example, the housing market in Asia growing rapidly resulting in increased demand for Canadian forestry products will increase demand for Canadian dollars (manufacturers need to purchase products with Canadian dollars). The increased demand for Canadian dollars would result in an increase in the price of the Canadian dollar, assuming the supply of money does not change.

In another example, if a corporation reports poor financial performance, investors who own stock in the company may decide to sell their common shares. The increased supply of the company's common shares in the marketplace would result in a decrease in the price of the shares.

There are different ways of valuing a nation's total production of goods and services — i.e., its output. Economic growth is an economy's ability to produce greater levels of output over time and is expressed as the percentage change in a nation's gross domestic product (GDP) over a given period. By measuring growth, we can better gauge the performance and overall health of the entire economy.

Gross domestic product is the market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given time period, usually a year or a quarter. The quarterly reports are used to keep track of the short-term activity within the market, while the annual reports are used to examine trends and changes in production and the standard of living.

Goods and services go through many stages of production before they end up in the hands of their final users. The calculation of GDP looks at the total amount of final goods produced over the period. A final good is a finished product, one that is purchased by the ultimate end user.

EXAMPLE: A computer is a final good, but the chip inside it is not since the chip was used to manufacture the computer. If the market value of all the chips were added together with the market value of all the computers, GDP would be overstated. Only the market value of the computer, a final good, is included in GDP.

textbook.section: The Three Approaches to Measuring GDP
textbook.content:
There are three ways of measuring GDP: the expenditure approach, the income approach, and the production approach.

Expenditure Approach: Adds up everything that consumers, businesses, and governments spend money on during a certain period. This approach measures GDP as the sum of four components:
1. Personal consumption (C)
2. Investment (I)
3. Government spending on goods and services (G)
4. Net exports (exports less imports) of goods and services (X − M)
GDP = C + I + G + (X − M)

Income Approach: Starts from the idea that total spending on goods and services should equal the total income generated by producing all of those goods and services. The GDP using the income approach is the sum of all income generated by this economic activity.

Production Approach: Also known as the value-added approach; calculates an industry or sector's output and subtracts the value of all goods and services used in production. Adding up the value-added contributions of the various economic sectors produces a country's total GDP.

These three measures of GDP show that all production results in income earned by workers, firms, or investors, and all production is eventually consumed (or stored as inventory). Thus, GDP is obtained by adding either all income or all value-added production. In theory, GDP measured by the three different approaches should be the same.

Producing more goods and services represents an improvement in a nation's standard of living. However, if the increase in GDP was simply the result of higher prices, then the cost of buying those goods and services has increased, which reflects an increase in our cost of living but not an improvement in living standards.

Nominal GDP is the value of all goods and services produced in a given year at prices that prevailed in that same year and is typically the amount reported in the financial press. Changes in nominal GDP from year to year reflect both changes in the prices of goods and services and changes in the volume of output produced.

Real GDP, or constant dollar GDP, is the dollar value of all goods and services produced in a given year valued at prices from a base year. Holding prices constant to this base year establishes a better measure of the change in GDP that is the result of changes in the amount of output produced during the year.

A doubling of GDP during the year tells us nothing about what is happening to the rate of real production unless we also know how prices or inflation also changed over the year. Therefore, differences between real and nominal GDP are entirely the result of changes in prices. Real GDP tells us what would have happened to spending on goods and services if quantities had changed but prices had not changed.

EXAMPLE: Assume the financial press reports that nominal GDP grew by 4.4% last year and prices rose by 1%. Is this a good outcome for the economy?

In nominal terms, the economy grew by 4.4%, which is a good amount of economic growth. However, when we adjust by 1% for the effect of rising prices, real economic growth was actually 3.4% (4.4% − 1%). The nation was more productive this year than last year.

However, what if the financial press reported that nominal GDP grew by 2.4% last year, whereas prices rose by 3.1%? In nominal terms, the economy grew 2.4%, but if we adjust by 3.1% for inflation, we see that the economy actually shrank by approximately 0.7% (2.4% − 3.1%). Real GDP growth therefore was negative, which means the nation was less productive last year than the year before.

textbook.section: Productivity and Determinants of Economic Growth
textbook.content:
Since the industrial revolution, the GDP of industrialized economies has tended to grow over time. Growth in GDP results from a variety of factors; among the more important are:
• Increases in population over time — even if the output of every worker remained constant, GDP would rise due to the growing workforce.
• Increases in the capital stock — as more workers are provided with additional equipment and as their skills have been improved with better training and education, individual productivity rises.
• Improvements in technology — technological innovation helps firms and workers to recombine existing resources of land, capital, and labour in new and increasingly productive ways.

Sustained growth compounds remarkably over time. A policy measure that increases annual growth from 2% to 3% doubles a nation's standard of living over a 20- to 40-year period.

The analysis of long-term trends in GDP growth rates is important, as it allows for the identification of countries with higher expected growth rates. If the analysis is correct, investment in these countries can lead to superior investment returns.

The determinants of economic growth (which are reflected in higher investment values) suggest:
• Capital accumulation alone cannot sustain growth. Eventually, increased capital leads to smaller and smaller gains in output.
• A higher savings rate is not responsible alone for sustained higher growth. Nonetheless, a higher savings rate can ultimately support a higher level of output per individual.
• Sustained growth requires technological progress which is associated with a complex pattern of basic research, applied research, and product development situated in a supportive entrepreneurial context.

textbook.section: What Are the Phases of the Business Cycle?
textbook.content:
Economic fluctuations present a recurring problem for policy makers as downturns in economic growth are directly related to rising unemployment. Such fluctuations in output and employment are called the business cycle, and directly affect the value of investments over time.

Growth in the economy is measured by the increase in real GDP. Even though the term cycle suggests that the business cycle is regular and predictable, this is not really the case. In reality, fluctuations in real output are both irregular and unpredictable, and this makes each business cycle unique. Nonetheless, the following sequence of events is relatively typical over the course of a business cycle:

Expansion: In times of normal growth, the economy is steadily expanding. An expansion is characterized by: stable inflation; businesses have adjusted inventories to meet higher demand and are investing in new capacity; rising corporate profits; new business start-ups outnumber bankruptcies; strong stock market activity; and steady job creation with the unemployment rate steady or falling. Overall, real GDP is rising during an expansion.

Peak: The top of the cycle. A peak is characterized by: demand begins to outstrip the capacity of the economy to supply it; labour and product shortages cause wage increases and inflation to rise; interest rates rise and bond prices fall (beginning to dampen business investment and reduce sales of houses and big-ticket consumer goods); business sales decline, resulting in accumulation of unwanted inventory and reduced profits; stock prices fall and stock market activity declines.

Contraction: When an economy passes its peak, it enters a downturn or contraction. If the contraction lasts at least two consecutive quarters, then the economy is considered to be in recession. A contraction is characterized by: the level of economic activity begins to decline (real GDP decreases); businesses faced with unwanted inventories and declining profits reduce production, postpone investment, curtail hiring and may lay off employees; business failures outnumber start-ups; falling employment erodes household income and confidence; consumers react by spending less and saving more, which further cuts into sales, fuelling the contraction.

Trough: As the contraction continues, falling demand and excess capacity curtail the ability of businesses to raise prices and of workers to demand higher salaries. The growth cycle reaches its lowest point. Characteristics: interest rates fall, triggering a bond rally; inflation falls; consumers who postponed purchases during the contraction are spurred by lower interest rates and begin to spend; stock prices rally.

Recovery: During the recovery, GDP returns to its previous peak. The recovery typically begins with renewed buying of interest rate-sensitive items like houses and cars. Businesses that reduced inventories during the contraction must increase production to meet the new demand. They are typically still too cautious to hire back significant numbers of workers, but the period of widespread layoffs is over. Unemployment remains high; wage pressures are restrained, and inflation may decline further. When the economy rises above its previous peak, another expansion has begun.

Economic indicators are statistics or data series that are used to analyze business conditions and current economic activity. They can help to show whether the economy is expanding or contracting. If certain key indicators suggest that the economy is going to do better in the future than had previously been expected, investors may decide to change their investment strategy.

Economic indicators are classified as leading, coincident, or lagging.

Leading Indicators: Leading indicators tend to peak and trough before the overall economy in anticipation of emerging trends in economic activity. They are the most useful and widely used of the economic indicators because they anticipate change by indicating what businesses and consumers have begun to produce and spend. Leading indicators include:
• Housing starts
• Manufacturers' new orders, which indicate expectations of higher levels of consumer purchases of such items as automobiles and appliances
• Commodity prices, which reflect rising or falling demand for raw materials
• Average hours worked per week, which rise or fall depending on the level of output and therefore anticipate changes in employment
• Stock prices, which suggest changing levels of profits
• The money supply, which indicates available liquidity and thus has an impact on interest rates

Coincident Indicators: Coincident indicators are those which change at approximately the same time and in the same direction as the whole economy, thereby providing information about the current state of the economy. Coincident indicators include: personal income, GDP, industrial production, and retail sales. Personal income is a good example because if it is rising, economic growth will typically follow.

Lagging Indicators: Lagging indicators are those that change after the economy as a whole has changed. These indicators are important because they can confirm that a business cycle pattern is occurring. Lagging indicators include: unemployment, private sector plant and equipment spending, business loans and interest on such borrowing, labour costs, and the inflation rate. Unemployment is one of the more popular lagging indicators because a rising unemployment rate is an indication that the economy is doing poorly or that companies are anticipating a downturn.

A popular definition of a recession is at least two consecutive quarters of declining real GDP. However, Statistics Canada and the U.S. National Bureau of Economic Research describe a recession differently.

Statistics Canada judges a recession by the depth, duration, and diffusion of the decline in business activity:
• The decline must be of substantial depth, since marginal declines in output may be merely statistical error.
• The duration must be more than a couple of months, since bad weather alone can cause a temporary decline in output.
• The decline must be a feature of the whole economy. While a strike in a major industry can cause GDP to decline, that does not constitute a recession.
• The behaviour of employment and per capita income may also be taken into account.

In recent years, the term soft landing has been used to describe a business cycle phase when economic growth slows sharply but does not turn negative, while inflation falls or remains low. Soft landings are considered the 'Holy Grail' of policy makers, who want sustained growth without the cost of recurring recessions.

Exhibit 3.1 | Recent Periods of Economic Slowdown and Recession in Canada
Dates                        | Duration
April 2001 – September 2001  | 5 months
July 2008 – July 2009        | 12 months
March 2020 – August 2020     | 6 months

In March 2020, Canada's real GDP fell by more than 7% as a result of the worldwide pandemic. Canada's GDP finished 2020 5.4% lower than the previous year. In the first quarter of 2021, Canada's real GDP growth rate was 1.38%. Canadian GDP has grown significantly since the second quarter of 2020.

textbook.section: What Are the Key Labour Market Indicators?
textbook.content:
For most Canadians, the performance of the economy affects them most personally in the labour market. When the economy is strong, so is the demand for labour: employment rises, the unemployment rate falls, and workers win bigger wage raises and/or non-wage benefits. Conversely, when the economy weakens, so does the demand for labour, and wage demands are restrained.

Statistics Canada divides the population into two groups: the working-age population (those individuals aged 15 years and older) and those too young to work. Statistics Canada also defines the labour force as the sum of the working-age population who are either employed or unemployed.

There are two key indicators that describe the labour market:
• The participation rate represents the share of the working-age population that is in the labour force. For Canada, the current participation rate is 65.4% (Source: Statistics Canada Labour Force Survey). The participation rate has followed a mostly upward trend over the last 50 years, rising from 54% in the early 1960s to its current level of 65.4%.
• The unemployment rate represents the share of the labour force that is unemployed and actively looking for work. The unemployment rate may rise either because the number of employed fell or the number of people entering the work force looking for work rose, or both. The unemployment rate in Canada was 5.8% in December 2023.

Canadian Unemployment Rate: Figure 3.3 shows the patterns in the Canadian unemployment rate since the 1960s. In general, the upward trend with large fluctuations corresponds to the trend and stages of the business cycle. Significant post-war peaks in unemployment were recorded during the last two major recessions in Canada: the peak at 11.9% corresponds to the recession of 1980–1983, while the peak of 11.4% corresponds to the recession of 1991. The sudden unemployment rate increase to 13.7% in early 2020 was a result of the worldwide pandemic. Unemployment finished the year at 8.8%. In December 2023, the unemployment rate has fallen to 5.8%.

There are four general types of unemployment: cyclical, seasonal, frictional, and structural.

Cyclical unemployment: Cyclical unemployment is tied directly to fluctuations in the business cycle. It rises when the economy weakens and firms lay off workers in response to lower sales. It drops when the economy strengthens again.

Seasonal unemployment: Seasonal unemployment occurs in industries that operate only during part of the year. For example, farmhands are hired to work from June to October. In the winter months, seasonal unemployment is a regular occurrence in such industries.

Frictional unemployment: Frictional unemployment is the result of normal labour turnover resulting from people entering and leaving the workforce and from the ongoing creation and destruction of jobs; people are looking for work because they have finished school, quit, been laid off, or been fired from their most recent job. This is a normal part of a healthy economy.

Structural unemployment: Structural unemployment occurs when workers are unable to find work or fill available jobs because they lack the necessary skills, do not live where jobs are available, or decide not to work at the wage rate offered by the market. This type of unemployment is closely tied to changes in technology, international competition, and government policy. Structural unemployment typically lasts longer than frictional unemployment because workers must retrain or possibly relocate to find a job.

The Natural Unemployment Rate: The existence of frictional and structural factors in the economy prevents unemployment from falling to zero. This minimal level of unemployment is called the natural unemployment rate. At this level of unemployment, the economy is thought to be operating at close to its full potential or capacity, such that all resources, including labour, are fully employed.

The Bank of Canada pays close attention to the actual unemployment rate and the natural unemployment rate, as the gap between the two has an important influence on wage inflation:
• When the actual unemployment rate is above the natural rate, an excess supply of workers in the market weakens labour bargaining power, which discourages wage gains and helps to keep inflation in check.
• When the actual unemployment rate is below the natural rate, a shortage of workers contributes to an increase in wage gains and higher inflation.

textbook.section: What Role Do Interest Rates Play?
textbook.content:
Interest rates are an important link between current and future economic activity. For consumers, interest rates represent the gain from deferring consumption from today to tomorrow via saving. For businesses, interest rates represent one component of the cost of capital — the cost of borrowing money. Thus, the rate of growth of the capital stock, which determines future output, is related to the current level of interest rates.

Interest rates are one of the most important financial variables affecting securities markets. Since they are essentially the price of credit, changes in interest rates reflect, and affect, the demand and supply for credit and debt, and this has direct implications for the bond and money markets. Changes in interest rates made by the Bank of Canada also signal changes in the direction of monetary policy, and this has broader implications for the entire economy.

Determinants of Interest Rates:
• Demand and supply of capital: A large government deficit or a boom in business investment raises the demand for capital and forces up the price of credit (interest rates). In turn, the higher interest rate may encourage people to save more. An increase in the savings of government, businesses, or consumers may reduce their demand for borrowing and, in turn, may reduce interest rates.
• Default risk: The greater the risk that borrowers may default on money they have borrowed, the higher the interest rate demanded by lenders. If the central government is at risk of defaulting on its debt, interest rates rise for everybody. This additional interest rate is referred to as a default premium.
• Foreign interest rates and the exchange rate: Since Canada has an open economy and investors are free to move their money between Canada and other countries, foreign interest rates and financial conditions influence Canadian interest rates.
• Central bank credibility: The central bank exercises its influence on the economy by raising and lowering short-term interest rates. One of its main responsibilities is to keep inflation low and stable. The more credible and long-established a commitment to low inflation has been, the lower interest rates will be to compensate for the risk of rising inflation.
• Inflation: The higher the expected inflation rate, the higher the interest rate that must be charged by lenders to compensate for the erosion of the purchasing power of money over the duration of the loan.

textbook.section: How Interest Rates Affect the Economy
textbook.content:
Higher interest rates affect the economy in the following ways:
• They may raise the cost of capital for business investments. An investment should earn a greater return than the cost of the funds used to make the investment. Higher interest rates reduce the possibility of profitable investments. In turn, this reduces business investment.
• By increasing the cost of borrowing, higher interest rates discourage consumers from spending, especially to buy houses and major durable goods like cars and furniture on credit. This encourages consumers to save more.
• By increasing the portion of household income needed to service debt, such as mortgage payments, they reduce the income available to be spent on other items. This effect may be offset somewhat by the higher interest income earned by savers.

Thus, higher interest rates have a negative effect on growth prospects. The effect of lower interest rates is the opposite in each case and can provide a positive environment for economic growth.

Expectations and Interest Rates: Investment decisions are forward-looking. Any decision to purchase a security is based on an expectation about the future return from the security. Increased optimism in the market can generate a rise in stock prices. Consumer pessimism can stall economic growth and decrease share prices. Moreover, government economic policies may work only through their impact on people's expectations. For example, the Bank of Canada makes considerable effort to maintain the credibility of its commitment to low inflation.

Nominal interest rate: the quoted rate on an investment, such as a Guaranteed Investment Certificate or Treasury bill. All other things equal, the higher the rate of inflation, the higher nominal interest rates will be.

Real interest rate: the nominal interest rate minus the expected inflation rate. If inflation expectations rise, real interest rates fall (assuming nominal rates don't adjust immediately).

Negative Interest Rates: A negative interest rate occurs when the interest rate charged on borrowed funds is less than zero. Under negative rates, banks may charge customers for keeping deposits in their accounts, or they may pay a customer to take a loan. Under negative interest rates, the borrower does not have to make any interim interest payments over the term of the debt. They make periodic payments related only to principal amortization and are required to repay only a discounted amount of the original loan at maturity.

textbook.section: What Is the Nature of Money and Inflation?
textbook.content:
Money is the essential ingredient that makes the economy function. Money can be any object that is accepted as payment for goods and services, and that can be used to settle debts:
• Its function as a medium of exchange is essential. Without money, goods and services would need to be exchanged with other goods and services in some form of barter system.
• Money also acts as a unit of account so that we know exactly the price of a good or service.
• Finally, money represents a store of value since it does not have an expiration date if a consumer decides to save it for a later use. The more stable the value of money, the better it can act as a store of value.

Inflation occurs when prices are rising. This is problematic because as prices rise, money begins to lose its value — more and more money is needed to buy the same amount of goods and services, and this has a negative effect on living standards. Inflation is an important economic indicator for securities markets because it is the rate at which the real value of an investment is eroded.

Inflation in an economy-wide sense is a generalized, sustained trend of rising prices:
• A one-time jump in prices caused by an increase in the price of oil or the introduction of a new sales tax is not true inflation unless it feeds into wages and other costs and initiates a wage-price spiral.
• A rise in the price of one product is not, in itself, a result of inflation; it may just be a relative price change reflecting the increased scarcity of that product.

Inflation is ultimately about money growth. It is a reflection of 'too much money chasing too few products.'

textbook.section: Measuring Inflation — The Consumer Price Index
textbook.content:
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is one of the most widely used indicators of inflation in Canada and is considered a measure of the cost of living in Canada. Statistics Canada tracks the retail price of a shopping basket comprised of 600 different goods and services, each weighted to reflect typical consumer spending. In this way, the CPI represents a measure of the average of the prices paid for this basket of goods and services.

Statistics Canada has a difficult task creating a basket of goods and services that is representative of the typical Canadian household. They try to make the relative importance of the items included in the CPI basket the same as that of an average Canadian household. However, it is almost impossible to construct a 'basket of goods' that would be representative of all consumers.

When calculating CPI, prices are measured against a base year, which at the moment is 2002 in Canada, and this base year is given a value of 100. The total CPI was 158.3 at the end of December 2023, which indicates that the basket of goods costs 58.3% more than it did in 2002.

The inflation rate is calculated by comparing the current period CPI with a previous period:

Inflation Rate = (CPI Current Period − CPI Previous Period) / CPI Previous Period × 100

The CPI was 158.3 in December 2023 and 153.1 in December 2022. The inflation rate over the 12-month period was:
Inflation Rate = (158.3 − 153.1) / 153.1 × 100 = 3.4%

The Inflation Rate in Canada: Inflation has not been much of a problem in Canada over the last decade. In recent history, Canada's inflation rate reached a high of 12.2% in 1981 and fell as low as −0.9% in July 2009. The inflation rate declined dramatically in both the early 1980s and 1990s based on monetary policy actions taken by the Bank of Canada.

textbook.section: Costs of Inflation and Causes of Inflation
textbook.content:
Inflation imposes many costs on the economy:
1. It erodes the standard of living of those on a fixed income and those who lack wage bargaining power. It rewards those able to increase their income either through wages or changes to their investment strategy.
2. Inflation reduces the real value of investments such as fixed-rate loans, since the loans are paid back in dollars that buy less. This can be good for the borrower if his or her income rises with inflation. But, more likely, inflation results in higher interest rates and lenders demanding a higher interest rate on money they lend.
3. Inflation distorts the signal prices send to participants in market economies, where prices are critical for balancing supply and demand. Rising prices draw resources into areas of scarcity; falling prices move funds away from glutted areas. When inflation is high, it is difficult to determine if a price increase is simply inflationary, or a genuine relative price change.
4. Accelerating inflation usually brings about rising interest rates and a recession. High-inflation economies usually experience more severe booms and busts than low-inflation economies.

The Causes of Inflation — The Output Gap:
Economists use an indicator called the output gap to measure inflation pressures in the economy by looking at the difference between real GDP (what the economy actually produces) and potential GDP (what the economy is capable of producing when all inputs of labour, capital, and technology are fully employed at their normal levels of use).

A negative output gap occurs when actual output is below potential output. In this case, economists would say there is spare capacity in the economy — the economy can produce more output because its resources are not being used to their full capacity. Unemployed workers and unused plant and equipment resources can be called into service without impacting wages or prices. Thus, inflation will fall or remain steady.

A positive output gap occurs when actual output is above potential output. In this case, economists would say the economy is operating above capacity — the economy is trying to produce more than it can with existing resources. Scarce labour fuels wage increases, and other strains on productive resources place upward pressure on inflation.

Demand-Pull Inflation: As the economy moves through an expansion toward the peak, output continues to expand, consumer income is rising, and this leads to strong consumer demand for goods and services. This creates a situation where companies can continue to operate well above normal capacity; strong demand means companies can raise their prices higher than normal. Higher and continued consumer demand pushes inflation higher. This state of affairs is called demand-pull inflation.

Cost-Push Inflation: Inflation can also rise or fall due to shocks from the supply side of the economy — when the cost of producing output changes. At a given price level, when faced with higher costs of production from higher wages or increases in the price of raw materials, firms respond by raising prices and producing a smaller amount of the product. In this way, the higher costs push inflation higher. This is an example of cost-push inflation.

Disinflation: Just as there are costs associated with rising inflation, a falling rate of inflation can also have a negative impact on the economy. Disinflation is a decline in the rate at which prices rise — i.e., a decrease in the rate of inflation. Prices are still rising, but at a slower rate.

The potential cost of disinflation is captured by the Phillips curve, which says that when unemployment is low, inflation tends to be high, and when unemployment is high, inflation tends to be low. According to this theory: lower unemployment is achieved in the short run by increasing inflation at a faster rate; lower inflation is achieved at the cost of possibly increased unemployment and slower economic growth.

To gauge the cost of disinflation, the sacrifice ratio is used to describe the extent to which GDP must be reduced to achieve a 1% decrease in the inflation rate. Experts suggest that the sacrifice ratio can be as high as 4; that is, 4% of output must be sacrificed to bring down inflation 1%. So, there may be a considerable cost in output to be paid in pursuing the goal of lower inflation. This cost could involve a significant period of relatively high unemployment.

Deflation in Canada: In 2007, the Bank Rate in Canada was as high as 4.75% (the Bank Rate is the rate of interest that the Bank of Canada charges on short-term loans to financial institutions). In response to the 2008–2009 recession, the Bank Rate fell to as low as 0.50% and remained there for more than a year to help stabilize the economy. For Canada, the recession was not as deep as expected and low interest rates played a key role in stimulating the Canadian economy.

Deflation: Deflation is a sustained fall in prices where the annual change in the CPI is negative year after year. In fact, deflation is just the opposite of inflation. Falling prices are generally good for the economy in the short term because goods and services become cheaper, and our income seems to go a little farther than it used to. Although true in the short term, there are negative consequences of deflation: as prices continue to fall, businesses must sell their products at lower prices, leading to declining corporate profits → businesses cut production costs and may cut wage rates → declining company earnings → economic slowdown → falling stock prices.

Monetary policy refers to regulation of the money supply and available credit for the purpose of promoting sustained economic growth and price stability. The goal of monetary policy is to maintain the value of our currency and our economic health.

Canada's central bank, the Bank of Canada, uses its influence over short-term interest rates to control changes in the money supply and available credit. The duties and role of the Bank are stated in a general way in the preamble of the Bank of Canada Act:
• 'To regulate credit and currency in the best interests of the economic life of the nation...'
• 'To control and protect the external value of the national monetary unit...'
• 'To mitigate by its influence fluctuations in the general level of production, trade, prices and employment, as far as may be possible within the scope of monetary action and generally...'
• 'To promote the economic and financial welfare of the Dominion.'

Monetary policy can be either expansionary or contractionary:
• An expansionary monetary policy increases the amount of money and credit available in the economy. EXAMPLE: If the Bank of Canada lowers the Bank Rate or buys bonds in the market, borrowing will become cheaper, and spending will be more attractive to consumers. As a result, more loans will be made, and less money will be tied up in investments. These activities will increase the money supply.
• A contractionary monetary policy decreases the amount of money and credit available in the economy. EXAMPLE: If the Bank of Canada increases the Bank Rate or sells bonds in the market, borrowing will become more expensive, and saving will be more attractive to consumers. As a result, fewer loans will be made, and more money will be tied up in investments. These activities will decrease the money supply.

Both expansionary and contractionary policies are implemented with the help of the Bank Rate and cash management techniques.

textbook.section: The Bank Rate and Cash Management
textbook.content:
The Bank Rate: The Bank of Canada carries out monetary policy primarily through changes to the target for the overnight rate.
